sentence: Samsung Galaxy S25 Edge review: More than just super thin
Mine arrives tomorrow to replace my S23 ultra.

On paper, this may feel like a downgrade to some...but I am looking so forward to having a phone that doesn't feel like a brick in my pocket and annoying to carry around...or having it fall on my face and leave a bruise when I'm using it laying in bed.

I've slowed down phone upgrades lately (used to be on the yearly bandwagon s7-s8-s9-s10+-s21u-s23u (only because the camera failed in the s21u)...but I've hated how heavy and unwieldy these big phones have become and have been pining for a high-end phone that's small and light.
Why choose the largest model?
Samsung annoyingly locks the best screen, cameras and storage out of the base phones-
I need at least 512GB storage.
Otherwise, I'd just choose the base phones with maxed out storage.
I still have my old S10+ in a drawer and took it out to inspect it....and man, that phone was amazing.
I didn't appreciate it at the time, but that phone is one of the all-time greats.
And I definitely can't believe the S25 edge will be thinner and lighter than that phone.
Looking forward to seeing if the S25 edge can recapture some of the S10+'s charm/magic.

The way I see it, and most others seem to do too, with all the complaints, is that manufacturers are ignoring the people's feedback and simply producing what "they want"..
The customer is what's keeping them alive, so that alone should drive the phones they produce, not trying to impress us with stuff we don't need.

I think a good move for phone manufacturing would be to see a yearly mandatory poll being publicly released on their website every year with points they plan to upgrade or improve on, maybe even trial new ideas there, before they jump the gun, to see what customers actually want and what they don't.
That way they can make informed and real changes, not gimmicks.
The most important factors these days that most seem to be crying out for are, bigger batteries, better cameras and more storage..
The rest is secondary.
Slim phones, AI in every corner, and the latest high end Snapdragon 8 Elite are no longer that important.
Slim is no good if you're squeezing a tiny battery in, when people want longevity.
Phone processors now are so good you don't need the latest and greatest.
They're fast enough for anyone and gimmicks just seem to annoy people.
AI, not everyone wants it so give people the choice between a model implemented with AI and one without, for a lesser cost on the latter.
Give people the choice instead of forcing it on everyone.

Also, technology advances seems to have slowed to an all time low because all we're seeing every year are tiny iterations, and they're struggling to produce anything with the "wow" effect, which is why they're resorting to gimmicks.
There's no real jumps anymore so it's not worth the upgrade every year, or even two.
Not like the good old days where every year was way better than the previous.
Maybe it's time for them to start releasing phones every 3 years rather than 10 different models a year.
That way we feel like we're actually getting the jump in tech we deserve and not flooding the market with dozens of wasteful makes and models of phones people don't want.

I mean, they /could/!
The way AirTags work is that they broadcast out a special token that is unrecognizable to any device other than the one paired with it (which must be an Apple device).
When an Apple device sees a token it doesn't recognize, it tells Apple's Find My Database "Hey, I found this token at this location."
Later, the owner of the AirTag can check the database for tokens they recognize to find the location of AirTags they own.
Google can't access that database because Apple only let's Apple Devices access it.
Google could create their own database of tokens, but that would be a waste of time because AirTags shuffle their tokens.
If Google somehow cracked the Find My protocol, it would be a huge security hole that Apple would fix (and Google probably wouldn't want to abuse ethically).
Also Google has it's own Find My equivalent network called Find Hub so it would be really weird for them to spend time trying to get AirTags to work when you could buy a Find Hub tag.
[ Here's a somewhat technical explanation of AirTag security:
Actually Apple and Google both have worked together to ensure that both users can detect tags that are following a user without the owner being present.
Once a tag is separated from the owner's device, it's marked lost.
Both iOS and Android can detect such tags.
This is to prevent stalking and spying.

Because it’s insanely problematic to expose that data to applications WiFi scan data is location data in disguise.
The list of visible BSSIDs and their signal strengths is a de facto fingerprint of your physical location.
So if a random app can scan nearby APs it can determine your location with reasonable accuracy without ever requesting location permission.

Since the set of networks visible from your home is unique...even without resolving it to GPS it’s a stable identifier and ad networks or analytics SDKs embedded in unrelated apps can use it to correlate users across apps or sessions without any traditional tracking identifier.
Don’t even need geolocation to do device tracking.
If an app can see the full list of networks you’ve been near (or probe requests your device sends) it can build a movement profile over time.
Retail analytics companies were doing this i.e tracking shoppers via WiFi probe requests and nearby AP scans.
Apple’s restriction closes that side channel.
What complete and utter made up garbage.
It's very clearly a word salad for people who have no clue how operating systems work.

The permission model easily takes care of access to BSSIDs, as well as historical lookup, and it's not the same as getting a location because the database for that lookup is proprietary and owned by the OS vendors.
Wardriving generated lookup databases are barely useful in populated city centers only.
And further this is unrelated to AP probing.
Which is unrelated to the original capability comment.
That's an individual app permission, "Nearby devices".
If an app asks for that permission that shouldn't need it don't give it to them.
Choice is better than no choice, than the gilded cage.
Informed consent doesn’t scale The “just don’t grant the permission 4head” argument assumes users understand what they’re consenting to.
when an app says “allow access to location,” almost nobody connects that to “this app can now fingerprint my physical position via WiFi BSSIDs and sell that to data brokers.”
The permission prompt is technically accurate but semantically opaque.
You’re not making an informed choice, you’re clicking through a dialog to use the app you already decided to install.
Studies consistently show that the vast majority of users grant whatever permissions are requested, especially when the app won’t function without them.
So the “choice” is largely illusory for the general population.
Then there’s the bundling problem.
A weather app legitimately needs location to show your forecast.
Under Android’s model, that same location permission also grants WiFi scan access.
The user didn’t choose to let the weather app map their home network topology, they chose to see local weather.

The permission granularity isn’t fine enough to separate “GPS for forecast” from “WiFi scanning for fingerprinting,” You’re not choosing between specific capabilities you’re flipping a switch that enables a bundle of things you didn’t evaluate individually.
There’s also an ecosystem externality argument.
Your choice to grant a permission doesn’t just affect you .
If a million people grant WiFi scan access to some flashlight app with an embedded analytics SDK, that SDK operator now has a massive database mapping BSSID clusters to locations.
That database can then be used to locate other people, including people who never installed that app and never consented to anything .
Your neighbor now affects you .
Individual choice doesn’t account for collective harm.
Then there’s the information asymmetry angle.
You’d need to reverse-engineer or audit every app to know whether it’s actually using WiFi scan data for legitimate purposes or quietly exfiltrating it.
Even if you’re technically capable of doing that (and you probably aren’t) expecting that of the general user base is asinine.
Developers have strong financial incentives to collect this data and weak accountability for misusing it.
People also say they deeply value privacy in surveys, but behaviorally they’ll accept almost any permission to use a free app.

If you design a system around the assumption that users will make careful permission decisions, and then observe that they empirically don’t, at some point you have to ask whether the system is serving its stated purpose or just providing legal cover for data collection.
Apple’s position is essentially: “we’ve seen what happens when you rely on user choice for this, it doesn’t work, so we’re removing the choice.”
You can disagree with the paternalism, but the I’d ask if you also disagree with building codes.

You could let every homebuyer individually assess whether the electrical wiring in a house is safe, but we don’t, we mandate standards because the cost of getting it wrong is high and the average person can’t meaningfully evaluate it.

I was watching some of the youtube video of this guy from Korea manually testing the Samsung Fold 7 for durability of the screen and hinge.
They called this a "real world test" versus using a machine to fold and unfold the phone.
I have a problem with either of these showing real world conditions.
particularly for the durability of the screen.

Both the cover plastic on the OLED screen, plus the OLED technology itself are going to be considerably more damaged by the continual folding and unfolding of the screen in the manner and speed which it is being done.
That speed is going to cause built up heat generation in the hinge, and in the plastics used in the screen due to internal stress.
In a normal real world use situation, the plastics and OLED screen technology would have time to cool, and "heal" between usages.
Many plastics have the ability to heal on a molecular level, reforming or straightening out the polymer chains while at rest, the same for screen technologies.
Even the "rigid" LCD screens can heal after a bang or some level of surface damage giving enough time, and sometimes a bit of a "finger massage".

I don't think it is fair to use that test, whether done by a machine (as some of the publications have done in the past) or the current manual test being done in Korea to represent a real world experience, and it is possible that given normal use, that the screen and hinge might last the 500K that Samsung claims.

I don't own any folding screen products, and I don't represent any personal interest in Samsung products, although I have bought a couple of their products over the years (tablets and TVs), so I have no personal axe to grind one way or the other, but fair is fair, and I don't think the current test in Korea is fair, if indeed, they are equating what they are doing with real world usage.
